title: Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita, 2024
Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to national estimates and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of workers in informal employment and GDP per capita for nations in 2024. The vertical axis measures the percentage of the workforce in informal jobs, ranging from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis plots GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale from $2,000 to $100,000. Circle sizes correspond to total population, with a reference scale showing 600 million and 1.4 billion. The data points form a distinct downward curve, showing that countries with lower GDP per capita consistently display higher shares of informal employment. At the upper end of the informal employment scale, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Chad, and Tanzania cluster near the 90% mark with GDP per capita figures below $2,000. India appears as a large circle in the upper-middle section, indicating a population over 1 billion with roughly 85% informal employment and a GDP per capita near $10,000. Other nations such as Pakistan, Ghana, and Indonesia also occupy the high-informality, lower-income quadrant. Moving toward the right side of the chart, countries with higher GDP per capita show significantly lower informal employment rates. South Korea, Spain, and Germany sit at the bottom right, with GDP per capita exceeding $40,000 and informal employment shares falling below 5%. Intermediate examples like Mexico, Argentina, and Turkey demonstrate the transition, with informal employment rates between 20% and 50% and GDP per capita ranging from $15,000 to $30,000. The employment definition includes the production of goods for own use, such as subsistence farming, and GDP figures are expressed in 2021 prices. The visible pattern shows a strong negative association where higher national income levels coincide with lower proportions of the workforce in informal jobs.
